docs.title: OpsForge
docs.text: Welcome to OpsForge, a consolidated toolkit designed for cybersecurity professionals and cloud infrastructure architects.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: OpsForge brings together 22 essential utilities into a single, monolithic CLI application, making it easier to audit, monitor, and secure your environments.

docs.title: Core Philosophy
docs.text:
• Deterministic: No AI-driven guesswork. All tools run predictable logic.
  • Offline First: Many tools work entirely offline without external API dependencies.
  • Unified Interface: One command to rule them all.

docs.title: Cybersecurity Utilities
docs.text:
• Port Scanner: Fast, socket-based scanning.
  • Log Analyzer: Forensic analysis of server logs.
  • Integrity Monitor: Cryptographic system file auditing.
  • ... and more.

docs.title: Cloud Utilities
docs.text:
• Tag Auditor: Ensure resource compliance.
  • IAM Linter: Find permissive security rules.
  • Infra Boilerplate: Generate standardized Terraform.
  • ... and more.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Check the Installation Guide to get OpsForge up and running on your system.

docs.title: Contributing to OpsForge
docs.text: We welcome contributions to OpsForge! Whether you're fixing a bug, improving documentation, or adding a completely new utility, your help is appreciated.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository on GitHub.
  2. Clone your fork locally: 
    git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/OpsForge.git
cd OpsForge
  3. Install in editable mode with development dependencies: 
    pip install -e .

docs.title: 1. Create the Module
docs.text: Add a new Python file in opsforge/modules/cyber/ or opsforge/modules/cloud/. Use the existing tools as a template, utilizing click for the CLI and rich for output.

docs.title: 2. Register the Command
docs.text: Update opsforge/main.py to import your module and add it to the appropriate command group (cyber or cloud).

docs.title: 3. Add Documentation
docs.text: Create a corresponding Markdown file in docs/cyber/ or docs/cloud/. Link it in the mkdocs.yml navigation section.

docs.title: Style Guidelines
docs.text:
• Use Type Hints for all function signatures.
  • Follow PEP 8 for Python code style.
  • Ensure all output uses the opsforge.core.output utilities for consistency.
  • Keep tools deterministic and avoid AI dependencies.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Please add unit tests for your changes in the tests/ directory and ensure they pass before submitting a Pull Request.

pytest

Thank you for helping make OpsForge better!

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: OpsForge is a Python-based CLI application. Follow these steps to install it on your system.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.8+
  • pip (Python package installer)
  • Git

docs.title: Standard Installation
docs.text:
You can install OpsForge directly from the repository:

git clone https://github.com/JoeMighty/OpsForge.git
cd OpsForge
pip install -e .

The -e flag installs the package in "editable" mode, which is recommended if you plan to update the tools frequently.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
Once installed, verify the installation by running:

opsforge --version

You should see output similar to: opsforge, version 0.1.0.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: OpsForge relies on several powerful libraries: - click: CLI framework. - rich: Beautiful terminal formatting. - scapy: Packet manipulation. - boto3: AWS SDK. - pyyaml: YAML parsing for config and pipelines.

docs.title: Cloud Infrastructure Utilities
docs.text: The Cloud module of OpsForge provides a suite of 11 tools designed for auditing, monitoring, and managing cloud infrastructure, with a primary focus on AWS.

docs.title: Available Tools
docs.text: Tool Functionality Tag Auditor Scans for missing mandatory resource tags. IAM Linter Evaluates IAM policies for permissive rules. Infra Generator Generates Terraform/CloudFormation templates. Stale Resource Finder Identifies idle or unattached cloud assets. NSG Rule Mapper Maps firewall rules to spreadsheets. Storage Auditor Verifies public storage exposure (S3). Image Inspector Analyzes Docker image layers. Uptime Monitor Pings endpoints for health checks. Pipeline Validator Linters for CI/CD YAML files. Cost Estimator Calculates projected costs offline. Drift Detector Compares code vs live infrastructure.

docs.title: Cloud Cost Estimator
docs.text: The Cloud Cost Estimator calculates projected monthly costs for common AWS resources using a built-in, offline pricing database. It is designed for quick, back-of-the-envelope calculations without requiring internet access or AWS API connectivity.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud cost-estimate [COMMAND] [OPTIONS]

docs.title: ec2
docs.text: Estimate monthly costs for EC2 instances. - -i, --instance TEXT: Instance type (e.g., t3.micro). - -c, --count INTEGER: Number of instances.

docs.title: s3
docs.text: Estimate monthly storage costs for S3. - -s, --size FLOAT: Storage size in GB. - -t, --tier [standard|infrequent]: Storage tier.

docs.title: list
docs.text: Lists all available pricing data in the offline database.

docs.title: Estimate cost for 5 micro instances
docs.text: opsforge cloud cost-estimate ec2 -i t3.micro -c 5

docs.title: Estimate cost for 1 TB of S3 storage
docs.text: opsforge cloud cost-estimate s3 --size 1000 --tier standard

docs.title: View all prices in the database
docs.text: opsforge cloud cost-estimate list

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses a hardcoded pricing dictionary (PRICING_DB) within the module. Prices are based on the US-East-1 region and are intended as approximations.

[!NOTE] For precise, up-to-the-minute pricing that includes data transfer, IOPS, and regional variations, always refer to the official AWS Pricing Calculator.

docs.title: Drift Detection Script
docs.text: The Drift Detection Script compares your local infrastructure-as-code state (Terraform terraform.tfstate file) against the live deployed resources in your cloud environment. It identifies resources that have been deleted manually via the console or CLI, creating a "drift" from the intended configuration.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud drift-detect [STATE_FILE]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • STATE_FILE: Path to the local JSON state file (e.g., terraform.tfstate).

docs.title: Detect drift in a production environment
docs.text: opsforge cloud drift-detect ./terraform.tfstate

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. It parses the provided JSON state file to extract a list of managed resources, their types, and their unique IDs.
  2. For supported resource types, it calls the corresponding AWS API (ec2:DescribeInstances, s3:HeadBucket, etc.) to verify if the resource still exists in the live environment.
  3. It reports the status:
  4. In-Sync: The resource exists in the live environment as expected.
  5. DRIFTED: The resource is defined in the state file but could not be found in the live environment (deleted).
  6. Skipped: The resource type is not yet supported for automated checking.

docs.title: Supported Resources
docs.text:
• aws_instance (EC2)
  • aws_s3_bucket (S3)
  • aws_security_group (NSG)

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses boto3 for live environment verification. It implements a read-only check and does not modify any live infrastructure or the state file itself.

[!IMPORTANT] This tool performs a existence check. It does not currently detect changes to individual resource attributes (e.g., if an instance type changed from t2.micro to m5.large). For full attribute-level drift detection, use the terraform plan command.

docs.title: IAM Policy Linter
docs.text: The IAM Policy Linter evaluates AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) JSON policy documents for security anti-patterns and overly permissive access rules. It helps enforce the Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP).

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud iam-lint [FILE]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • FILE: Path to the IAM policy JSON file to audit.

docs.title: Audit a local policy file
docs.text: opsforge cloud iam-lint my_policy.json

docs.title: Security Checks
docs.text: The tool scans for the following high-risk patterns: - Action Wildcard (*): Grants access to all actions within a service or across all services if used as *. This is flagged as CRITICAL. - Resource Wildcard (*): Grants access to all resources. This is often necessary but should be scrutinized for sensitive services. Flagged as WARNING. - Dangerous Actions: Specifically looks for high-impact actions like iam:*, s3:DeleteBucket, and ec2:TerminateInstances. Flagged as IMPORTANT.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool is an offline validator. It does not communicate with the AWS API; it parses and analyzes the provided JSON file locally using logic-based heuristics.

[!CAUTION] An IAM policy that passes this linter is not necessarily "secure" in your specific context, but it is free from common, glaring security flaws. Always perform a manual review for mission-critical policies.

docs.title: Container Image Inspector
docs.text: The Container Image Inspector analyzes Docker images to identify excessively large layers and retrieve detailed metadata. It helps developers and DevOps engineers optimize image sizes and understand the build history of a container.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud image-inspect [IMAGE_NAME]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • IMAGE_NAME: The name or ID of the local Docker image (e.g., nginx:latest).

docs.title: Inspect a local image
docs.text: opsforge cloud image-inspect my-app:v1

docs.title: Data Extracted
docs.text:
• Layer History: Breaks down the image into its constituent layers, showing the size of each layer and the Dockerfile command (CreatedBy) that generated it.
  • Architecture/OS: Displays the target CPU architecture (e.g., amd64) and operating system.
  • Author: Shows the image author/maintainer if specified in the metadata.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool acts as a wrapper around the docker inspect and docker history commands. It parses the resulting JSON and formatted output to provide a clean, readable summary in the terminal.

[!IMPORTANT] Prerequisites: This tool requires the Docker Desktop or Docker Engine to be installed and running on the local system. The image must also be available locally (i.e., you must run docker pull first if the image is in a remote registry).

docs.title: Optimization Tips
docs.text:
• Multi-stage builds: Use multi-stage builds to keep the final image small by excluding build-time dependencies.
  • Combine RUN commands: Combine multiple apt-get or pip install commands into a single RUN instruction to reduce the number of layers.
  • Clear Caches: Always clear package manager caches (e.g., rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*) in the same layer they were created.

docs.title: Infra Boilerplate Generator
docs.text: The Infra Boilerplate Generator outputs standardized, best-practice infrastructure-as-code templates (Terraform). It uses Jinja2 templating to inject project-specific variables into a consistent base architecture.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud infra-gen [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -t, --type [terraform]: Type of infrastructure code to generate. [default: terraform]
  • -p, --project TEXT: Project name (used for resource naming). [default: opsforge-project]
  • -r, --region TEXT: Target AWS region. [default: us-east-1]
  • --vpc-cidr TEXT: CIDR block for the VPC. [default: 10.0.0.0/16]
  • -o, --output PATH: Output filename. [default: main.tf]
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Generate a standard Terraform baseline
docs.text: opsforge cloud infra-gen -p my-app -r eu-central-1

docs.title: Generated Resources
docs.text: The default Terraform template includes: - VPC: A VPC with the specified CIDR block and project tags. - S3 Bucket: A private data bucket with force_destroy enabled. - S3 Public Access Block: Strict blocking of all public access to the S3 bucket to ensure security by default.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses Jinja2 to render .j2 templates stored within the package. The logic is entirely local and deterministic, ensuring that the same inputs always result in the same boilerplate code.

[!TIP] Use this tool to quickly initialize new cloud projects with security-first defaults (like private S3 buckets) instead of starting from scratch.

docs.title: NSG Rule Mapper
docs.text: The NSG Rule Mapper extracts and flattens cloud firewall rules (AWS Security Groups) into a readable format. It helps security auditors quickly visualize the ingress and egress rules across a large number of security groups.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud nsg-map [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -r, --region TEXT: AWS region to scan. [default: us-east-1]
  • -o, --output PATH: Output CSV filename to save all rules.
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Preview rules in the console
docs.text: opsforge cloud nsg-map

docs.title: Export all rules to a CSV file
docs.text: opsforge cloud nsg-map -o sg_audit.csv

docs.title: Data Extracted
docs.text: For every security group rule, the tool extracts: - SG Name: The descriptive name of the Security Group. - SG ID: The unique AWS identifier for the Security Group. - Protocol: The IP protocol (TCP, UDP, ICMP, or All). - Ports: The port or port range allowed. - Source: The source CIDR block or the ID of the source Security Group.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses boto3 to call ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups. It flattens the nested IpPermissions structure into a linear list of rules, making it much easier to analyze in a spreadsheet or table.

[!NOTE] This tool currently focuses on Ingress (inbound) rules, as they represent the primary attack surface.

docs.title: CI Pipeline Validator
docs.text: The CI Pipeline Validator parses YAML configuration files for popular CI/CD platforms (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) to ensure they adhere to organizational testing standards and security best practices.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud pipeline-val [FILE]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • FILE: Path to the CI YAML file (e.g., .github/workflows/deploy.yml).

docs.title: Validate a GitHub Actions workflow
docs.text: opsforge cloud pipeline-val .github/workflows/main.yml

docs.title: Validate a GitLab CI file
docs.text: opsforge cloud pipeline-val .gitlab-ci.yml

docs.title: Platform Detection
docs.text: Automatically identifies if the file is a GitHub Actions workflow or a GitLab CI configuration based on the top-level keys (jobs vs stages).

docs.title: Automated Testing Check
docs.text: Verifies that the pipeline contains at least one job or stage dedicated to testing (checks for keywords like test or check).

docs.title: Secret Security
docs.text: Performs a heuristic scan of run commands to identify potential hardcoded secrets (passwords, tokens). It flags commands that contain sensitive keywords but do not use the platform's native secret injection syntax (e.g., ${{ secrets.VAR }}).

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses PyYAML for deterministic parsing of the configuration files and applies a set of rule-based heuristics to the resulting data structure.

[!WARNING] This linter provides a "baseline" check. It is not a replacement for comprehensive security tools like Checkov or Gitleaks, but rather a quick way to ensure developers haven't bypassed basic testing requirements.

docs.title: Stale Resource Finder
docs.text: The Stale Resource Finder identifies unattached or idle cloud assets in your AWS environment. Finding and removing these resources is a key part of cloud cost optimization and security hygiene.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud stale-find [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -r, --region TEXT: AWS region to scan. [default: us-east-1]
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Scan for stale resources in the default region
docs.text: opsforge cloud stale-find

docs.title: Scan a different region
docs.text: opsforge cloud stale-find -r eu-west-1

docs.title: 1. Unattached EBS Volumes
docs.text: Scans for EC2 Elastic Block Store (EBS) volumes with a status of available. These volumes are not attached to any running or stopped instances and are incurring costs.

docs.title: 2. Unassociated Elastic IPs
docs.text: Identifies Elastic IP addresses (EIPs) that are not associated with any instance or network interface. AWS charges a small hourly fee for unassociated EIPs.

docs.title: 3. Idle Load Balancers (ALB/NLB)
docs.text: Checks Application and Network Load Balancers to see if they have any targets registered in their target groups. An ALB with no targets is usually an abandoned resource that should be deleted.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses boto3 to query the EC2 and ELBv2 APIs. It filters for specific resource states that indicate a "stale" or "idle" condition.

[!TIP] Run this tool monthly as part of your "Cloud Cleanup" routine to significantly reduce unnecessary cloud spend.

docs.title: Public Storage Auditor
docs.text: The Public Storage Auditor scans S3 buckets in your AWS account to verify they are not publicly exposed. Publicly readable S3 buckets are one of the most common causes of massive data breaches in the cloud.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud storage-audit

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: The tool performs a two-layer security check for every bucket in the account:

docs.title: 1. Public Access Block (PAB)
docs.text: Checks if the S3 Block Public Access feature is enabled. This is the recommended "master switch" to prevent accidental exposure. - Locked: All four PAB settings are enabled (Best Practice). - Partial Block: Some PAB settings are disabled. - No Block Config: PAB is not configured, meaning the bucket relies solely on ACLs and Policies.

docs.title: 2. Bucket ACLs
docs.text: Analyzes the Access Control List (ACL) for each bucket to see if access has been granted to: - AllUsers: Anyone on the internet. - AuthenticatedUsers: Any AWS user (not just those in your account).

docs.title: Run account-wide audit
docs.text: opsforge cloud storage-audit

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses boto3 to call s3:ListBuckets, s3:GetPublicAccessBlock, and s3:GetBucketAcl. It does not currently audit complex Bucket Policies, as those require more sophisticated semantic analysis.

[!CAUTION] If a bucket is flagged as EXPOSED, immediate action is required. Enable "Block Public Access" via the AWS Console or CLI to secure the data.

docs.title: Resource Tagging Auditor
docs.text: The Resource Tagging Auditor scans AWS assets to identify resources that are missing mandatory billing or ownership tags. This is essential for cost allocation, security ownership, and organizational compliance.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud tag-audit [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -r, --region TEXT: AWS region to scan. [default: us-east-1]
  • -m, --mandatory TEXT: Comma-separated list of mandatory tag keys. [default: Project,Owner,Environment]
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Scan default region with default tags
docs.text: opsforge cloud tag-audit

docs.title: Scan a specific region with custom mandatory tags
docs.text: opsforge cloud tag-audit -r us-west-2 -m CostCenter,Dept,AppID

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool utilizes the AWS Resource Groups Tagging API (get_resources) via boto3. It iterates through all supported resources in the specified region and compares their attached tags against the provided mandatory list.

[!IMPORTANT] Authentication: This tool requires valid AWS credentials configured on your system (e.g., via ~/.aws/credentials or environment variables like AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID). Permissions: The IAM identity used must have the tag:GetResources permission.

docs.title: Why Tagging Matters?
docs.text:
• Billing: Categorize costs by project or environment.
  • Automation: Target specific resources for automated backup or patching.
  • Security: Identify which team or individual is responsible for a specific asset.

docs.title: Uptime Health Monitor
docs.text: The Uptime Health Monitor pings cloud endpoints (URLs) at regular intervals to verify their availability. It alerts the user immediately if any endpoint returns a non-200 status code or becomes unreachable.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cloud uptime-monitor [URLS...] [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • URLS: One or more URLs to monitor (space-separated).

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -i, --interval INTEGER: Interval between checks in seconds. [default: 60]
  • -c, --count INTEGER: Number of checks to perform (0 for infinite). [default: 1]
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Single check of multiple endpoints
docs.text: opsforge cloud uptime-monitor api.myapp.com auth.myapp.com

docs.title: Continuous monitoring every 30 seconds
docs.text: opsforge cloud uptime-monitor https://status.myapp.com -i 30 -c 0

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: The tool uses the requests library to send HTTP GET requests to each provided URL. - Success: Status code 200 OK. - Warning/Critical: Any other status code (e.g., 404, 500, 503). - Down: Connection timeout or resolution failure.

docs.title: Metrics Recorded
docs.text:
• Status Code: The HTTP response code from the server.
  • Latency: The time taken (in milliseconds) to receive the response.
  • Timestamp: The exact time the check was performed.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool runs in a simple loop on the main thread. It is designed for lightweight, manual monitoring or for use in background terminal sessions.

[!TIP] For production environments, always complement this tool with managed services like AWS Route53 Health Checks or CloudWatch Synthetics.

docs.title: Cybersecurity Utilities
docs.text: The Cyber module of OpsForge provides a suite of 11 tools designed for offensive and defensive security operations.

docs.title: Available Tools
docs.text: Tool Functionality Port Scanner Probes IP addresses for open ports. Log Analyzer Parses server logs for malicious patterns. Integrity Monitor Audits system file hashes. Subdomain Enumerator Maps external attack surfaces via DNS. Password Entropy Evaluates password strength offline. Packet Sniffer Captures local network traffic. Encoder/Decoder Payload transformation (Base64, Hex, etc). Directory Discovery Bruteforces web server directories. SSL Auditor Checks certificate health and crypto. Honeypot Listener Deceptive service for capturing IP logs. Vulnerability Fuzzer Injects test strings into URLs.

docs.title: Directory Discovery
docs.text: The Directory Discovery tool automates HTTP requests to find hidden web server directories and files by brute-forcing against a wordlist.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber dir-discover [URL] [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • URL: The base URL of the target web server (e.g., http://example.com).

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -w, --wordlist PATH: Path to a custom directory wordlist.
  • -t, --threads INTEGER: Number of concurrent threads. [default: 10]
  • -e, --extensions TEXT: File extensions to check (comma-separated, e.g., .php,.html,.bak).
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Quick discovery with defaults
docs.text: opsforge cyber dir-discover http://scanning.me

docs.title: Discovery with custom wordlist and extensions
docs.text: opsforge cyber dir-discover 10.0.0.5 -w common_dirs.txt -e .php,.txt,.json -t 50

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool performs synchronous HTTP requests using the requests library. It uses a ThreadPoolExecutor to handle multiple requests in parallel. For efficiency, it first sends an HTTP HEAD request; if the server responds with a status code other than 404, it marks the item as found.

[!NOTE] Extensive directory brute-forcing generates significant log noise on the target web server and may be blocked by Web Application Firewalls (WAFs). Use with caution and only on authorized targets.

docs.title: Payload Encoder/Decoder
docs.text: The Payload Encoder/Decoder converts strings between common formats used in web applications and security testing: Base64, Hex, and URL encoding.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber encode-decode [TYPE] [DATA] [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
• base64: Base64 encoding/decoding.
  • hex: Hexadecimal encoding/decoding.
  • url: URL (percent) encoding/decoding.

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -d, --decode: Perform decoding instead of encoding.
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Base64 encode a string
docs.text: opsforge cyber encode-decode base64 "secret_payload"

docs.title: Base64 decode a string
docs.text: opsforge cyber encode-decode base64 "c2VjcmV0X3BheWxvYWQ=" -d

docs.title: URL encode a string
docs.text: opsforge cyber encode-decode url "admin' OR 1=1 --"

docs.title: Hex encode a string
docs.text: opsforge cyber encode-decode hex "Hello World"

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses Python's standard libraries (base64, urllib.parse, and built-in hex methods) to ensure deterministic and accurate transformations.

[!TIP] Use URL encoding when crafting payloads for web forms or URL parameters to ensure special characters are handled correctly by the server.

docs.title: Vulnerability Fuzzer
docs.text: The Vulnerability Fuzzer is a simple automated tool that injects common attack payloads into URL query parameters to identify potential validation flaws, such as SQL Injection, Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), or Local File Inclusion (LFI).

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber fuzzer [URL] [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • URL: The target URL to fuzz (e.g., http://example.com/search).

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -p, --payloads PATH: Path to a file containing custom payloads (one per line).
  • --param TEXT: The query parameter to inject payloads into. [default: id]
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Basic fuzzing on the 'id' parameter
docs.text: opsforge cyber fuzzer http://scanning.me/profile

docs.title: Fuzzing a specific 'search' parameter with custom payloads
docs.text: opsforge cyber fuzzer http://scanning.me/find -p xss_payloads.txt --param search

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. It iterates through a list of payloads (either defaults or from a provided file).
  2. It sends an HTTP GET request to the target URL for each payload, injecting the payload into the specified query parameter.
  3. It records the HTTP status code and response body size.

docs.title: Analyzing Results
docs.text: Look for anomalies in the output table: - Status 500 (Internal Server Error): Often indicates a crash or unhandled exception on the server, which could be a sign of a vulnerability (especially SQLi). - Status 200 with unusual response size: Significant changes in response size compared to other payloads might indicate that the payload successfully changed the page content (e.g., successful XSS injection or LFI).

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses the requests library to perform HTTP operations. It is designed for basic, deterministic vulnerability probing.

[!WARNING] This tool performs "active" probing and will be logged by the target web server. Use only on systems you own or have explicit permission to test.

docs.title: Local Honeypot Listener
docs.text: The Local Honeypot Listener opens deceptive local services on specified ports. It logs every connection attempt, including the source IP and timestamp, providing an early warning system for internal network scanning or unauthorized access attempts.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber honeypot [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -p, --ports TEXT: Comma-separated list of ports to listen on. [default: 21,22,23,80,443,3306]
  • -o, --output PATH: Log file to store connection alerts. [default: honeypot.log]
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Start with default ports
docs.text: opsforge cyber honeypot

docs.title: Listen on custom sensitive ports
docs.text: opsforge cyber honeypot -p 1433,1521,3389,5900 -o intrusions.log

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: The tool initializes TCP listeners on all specified ports using multiple threads. When a connection is established: 1. It logs the source IP and timestamp. 2. It displays a real-time "ALARM" in the console. 3. It sends a generic service banner (e.g., a fake SSH or FTP header) to entice the scanner/attacker into revealing more information. 4. It immediately closes the connection.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This utility uses Python's socket module for raw networking and threading to manage simultaneous listeners across different ports. It is designed to be lightweight and purely deterministic.

[!IMPORTANT] Port Conflicts: This tool cannot listen on ports that are already in use by legitimate services on your machine. Privileges: Listening on well-known ports (below 1024) typically requires administrator/root privileges.

docs.title: File Integrity Monitor
docs.text: The File Integrity Monitor (FIM) calculates and compares cryptographic hashes (SHA-256) of local system files to detect unauthorized changes, deletions, or new file additions.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber integrity-check [COMMAND] [ARGS]

docs.title: generate
docs.text:
Creates a fingerprint (JSON file) containing hashes of all files in a directory.

opsforge cyber integrity-check generate [DIRECTORY] [OPTIONS]
- DIRECTORY: Path to the directory to fingerprint. - -o, --output TEXT: Output filename. [default: fingerprint.json]

docs.title: verify
docs.text:
Compares a directory against an existing fingerprint file.

opsforge cyber integrity-check verify [DIRECTORY] [FINGERPRINT_FILE]
- DIRECTORY: Path to the directory to verify. - FINGERPRINT_FILE: Path to the JSON fingerprint file.

docs.title: Baseline a system directory
docs.text: opsforge cyber integrity-check generate /etc/nginx -o nginx_baseline.json

docs.title: Verify integrity later
docs.text: opsforge cyber integrity-check verify /etc/nginx nginx_baseline.json

docs.title: Detection Capabilities
docs.text:
• MODIFIED: Files whose content has changed (different SHA-256 hash).
  • MISSING: Files that were in the baseline but are now gone.
  • NEW: Files that exist in the directory but were not in the baseline.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool implements the SHA-256 algorithm via Python's hashlib. It reads files in 4096-byte blocks to ensure memory efficiency even when hashing large files.

[!WARNING] Ensure the baseline fingerprint file is stored in a secure, read-only location or on a separate system to prevent attackers from tampering with the baseline itself.

docs.title: Access Log Analyzer
docs.text: The Access Log Analyzer parses server logs (Apache/Nginx) to flag repeated failed logins, traffic spikes, and potential web attacks like SQL Injection or XSS.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber log-analyze [LOGFILE] [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • LOGFILE: Path to the server log file (e.g., access.log).

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• --threshold INTEGER: Threshold for flagging IPs (failed logins or request count). [default: 10]
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Standard analysis
docs.text: opsforge cyber log-analyze /var/log/nginx/access.log

docs.title: Analysis with lower threshold
docs.text: opsforge cyber log-analyze access.log --threshold 5

docs.title: High Traffic
docs.text: Identifies IPs making an unusually high number of requests within the log file. This can help identify scraping bots or potential DoS attempts.

docs.title: Failed Logins
docs.text: Scans for 401 (Unauthorized) and 403 (Forbidden) status codes associated with specific IPs. Multiple failures from a single IP often indicate a brute-force attempt.

docs.title: Web Attacks
docs.text: Uses regex patterns to find common attack strings in URL fragments: - SQL Injection: SELECT, UNION, INSERT, etc. - XSS: <script>, alert(, etc.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
The tool uses Python's re (Regular Expression) module to parse log lines according to the Combined Log Format. It uses collections.Counter for efficient frequency tracking.

[!TIP] Run this tool periodically via a cron job and pipe the output to a file or alert system for continuous monitoring.

docs.title: Packet Sniffer Utility
docs.text: The Packet Sniffer Utility captures and decodes local network traffic headers and payloads in real-time. It is built on the powerful scapy library.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber packet-sniff [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -i, --iface TEXT: Interface to sniff on (e.g., eth0, wlan0, en0).
  • -c, --count INTEGER: Number of packets to capture. [default: 10]
  • -f, --filter TEXT: BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) string (e.g., 'tcp and port 80').
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Capture 10 packets on default interface
docs.text: opsforge cyber packet-sniff

docs.title: Capture HTTP traffic on a specific interface
docs.text: opsforge cyber packet-sniff -i eth0 -f "tcp port 80" -c 50

docs.title: Capture only UDP traffic
docs.text: opsforge cyber packet-sniff -f "udp"

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses Scapy to interface with the network stack. It decodes standard IP, TCP, and UDP headers and attempts to display the raw payload data (in hex format) if available.

[!WARNING] Privileged Access Required: This tool typically requires administrative or root privileges to access the network interface in promiscuous mode. On Windows, ensure you are running the terminal as Administrator. On Linux/macOS, use sudo.

docs.title: BPF Filter Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
• tcp: Capture only TCP packets.
  • udp: Capture only UDP packets.
  • port 80: Capture traffic to/from port 80.
  • host 1.1.1.1: Capture traffic to/from a specific IP.
  • net 192.168.1.0/24: Capture traffic for an entire subnet.

docs.title: Password Entropy Checker
docs.text: The Password Entropy Checker evaluates the mathematical strength of passwords offline. It calculates the Shannon entropy to estimate how many bits of information the password contains.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber password-check [PASSWORD] [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • PASSWORD: The password string to evaluate (optional if using interactive mode).

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -i, --interactive: Prompt for the password securely (prevents the password from appearing in shell history).
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Quick check
docs.text: opsforge cyber password-check "P@ssw0rd123!"

docs.title: Secure interactive check (Recommended)
docs.text: opsforge cyber password-check -i

docs.title: Understanding Entropy
docs.text:
Entropy is measured in bits. It represents the number of guesses required to crack a password in a worst-case brute-force scenario ($2^E$ guesses).

Entropy Strength < 40 bits Very Weak 40-60 bits Weak 60-80 bits Moderate 80-100 bits Strong > 100 bits Very Strong

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses the formula $E = L \times \log_2(R)$, where: - $L$ is the length of the password. - $R$ is the size of the character pool (charset) used in the password.

It automatically detects the charset size based on the presence of lowercase, uppercase, digits, and special characters.

[!IMPORTANT] This tool runs entirely offline. Your password is never sent to any server or API.

docs.title: Network Port Scanner
docs.text: The Network Port Scanner probes IP addresses for open ports using raw socket connections. It is a high-speed, multi-threaded utility designed for rapid surface mapping.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber port-scan [TARGET] [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • TARGET: The IP address or hostname to scan.

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -p, --ports TEXT: Port range (e.g., 80,443 or 1-1024). [default: 1-1024]
  • -t, --threads INTEGER: Number of concurrent threads. [default: 50]
  • --timeout FLOAT: Socket timeout in seconds. [default: 1.0]
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Scan common ports
docs.text: opsforge cyber port-scan 192.168.1.1

docs.title: Scan specific ports
docs.text: opsforge cyber port-scan 10.0.0.5 -p 22,80,443,3306

docs.title: Scan a large range with high concurrency
docs.text: opsforge cyber port-scan scanning.me -p 1-65535 -t 200

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses Python's native socket library to attempt TCP connections. It uses a ThreadPoolExecutor to handle multiple connection attempts simultaneously without blocking the main thread.

[!NOTE] This tool performs a "Full Connect" scan, which is detectable by most Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS).

docs.title: SSL Certificate Auditor
docs.text: The SSL Certificate Auditor retrieves and analyzes SSL/TLS certificates for a given hostname. It checks expiration dates, issuer authenticity, and the strength of the cryptographic protocols and ciphers in use.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber ssl-audit [HOSTNAME] [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • HOSTNAME: The domain or IP to audit (e.g., google.com).

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -p, --port INTEGER: Port to check. [default: 443]
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Audit a standard web server
docs.text: opsforge cyber ssl-audit google.com

docs.title: Audit a custom port
docs.text: opsforge cyber ssl-audit local-dev.server -p 8443

docs.title: Metrics Audited
docs.text:
• Status: Whether the certificate is currently valid or expired.
  • Expiration: The exact date/time the certificate expires and the remaining days.
  • Protocol: The TLS version in use (e.g., TLSv1.3).
  • Cipher: The encryption algorithm negotiated for the connection.
  • Issuer: The Certificate Authority (CA) that signed the certificate.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses Python's native ssl and socket modules. It performs a standard TLS handshake to retrieve the server's public certificate and connection metadata. It does not require external binary dependencies like OpenSSL to be installed on the system path.

[!TIP] Always ensure your servers support TLSv1.2 or TLSv1.3 and disable older, insecure protocols like SSLv3 or TLSv1.0.

docs.title: Subdomain Enumerator
docs.text: The Subdomain Enumerator maps a target domain's external attack surface by attempting to resolve common subdomains via DNS queries.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: opsforge cyber subdomain-enum [DOMAIN] [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: • DOMAIN: The base domain to enumerate (e.g., google.com).

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
• -w, --wordlist PATH: Path to a custom subdomain wordlist.
  • -t, --threads INTEGER: Number of concurrent threads. [default: 20]
  • --help: Show this message and exit.

docs.title: Quick enumeration with defaults
docs.text: opsforge cyber subdomain-enum example.com

docs.title: Enumeration with a large wordlist
docs.text: opsforge cyber subdomain-enum target.org -w subdomains_top_5000.txt -t 100

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
This tool uses a wordlist-based approach (brute-forcing) to identify valid subdomains. It leverages socket.gethostbyname() for DNS resolution and ThreadPoolExecutor for high-concurrency scanning.

[!NOTE] This tool only performs DNS lookups. It does not interact with the target web servers directly, making it a "passive" discovery tool in terms of HTTP traffic.
